title: The Market Hall at Chipping Campden
summary: Built in 1627 by Sir Baptist Hicks, the Market Hall sheltered traders selling butter, cheese, and poultry. It remains one of the finest examples in the Cotswolds.
body: The Market Hall was built in 1627 by the wealthy merchant Sir Baptist Hicks as a shelter for market traders. Its open arches allowed goods — mainly butter, cheese, and poultry — to be sold in all weathers. Hicks, who made his fortune in the London silk trade, was a great benefactor to the town. The Hall was given to the National Trust in 1942 and remains one of the most photographed buildings in the Cotswolds. It stands at the start of the Cotswold Way, marking the beginning of many walkers' journeys south to Bath.

title: The Cotswold Wool Trade
summary: The golden stone buildings you see across the Cotswolds were funded by medieval wool wealth. Cotswold sheep produced the finest fleece in Europe.
body: Between the 13th and 16th centuries, the Cotswolds was one of the wealthiest regions in England. Cotswold Lion sheep produced fine, heavy fleeces that were exported across Europe. The 'wool churches' built with this wealth — at Chipping Campden, Northleach, and Cirencester — are among the finest parish churches in England. The honey-coloured limestone buildings that define the Cotswold landscape were largely built during this prosperous era. Even the name 'Chipping' comes from the Old English 'ceaping', meaning market.

title: Broadway Tower: A Folly with a View
summary: Built in 1798 for Lady Coventry, who wanted to know if a beacon on this hilltop could be seen from her home 22 miles away. It could.
body: Broadway Tower was designed by James Wyatt and built in 1798 for Lady Coventry of Croome Court in Worcestershire. She wanted to know whether a beacon lit on this hilltop — the second highest point in the Cotswolds — could be seen from her home 22 miles away. It could. The tower has since served as a retreat for William Morris and the Arts and Crafts movement, a Cold War nuclear bunker observation post, and is now a popular tourist attraction. On a clear day, 16 counties are visible from the top.

title: Broadway: Artists' Village
summary: In the 1880s, Broadway became a colony for American and British artists, drawn by the golden light and picturesque cottages.
body: In the 1880s, Broadway became an artists' colony when the American painter Francis Davis Millet settled there. He was soon joined by John Singer Sargent, Edwin Austin Abbey, and other leading artists of the day. The village's wide, tree-lined main street, honey-coloured buildings, and soft Cotswold light made it irresistible. Sargent painted his famous 'Carnation, Lily, Lily, Rose' in the garden of Farnham House here. The tradition continues today with the Gordon Russell Design Museum and numerous galleries.

title: Stanway's Gravity Fountain
summary: Stanway House has the tallest gravity-fed fountain in the world at 300 feet, powered entirely by the natural fall of water from the hillside above.
body: Stanway House, a magnificent Jacobean manor, is home to the tallest gravity-fed fountain in the world. Rising to 300 feet, the fountain is powered entirely by a natural water supply from the hills above — no pumps needed. The house has been in the same family, the Earls of Wemyss, since 1533. Its gatehouse is considered one of the finest in England, and the tithe barn dates from the 14th century. The cricket ground in the grounds is one of the most scenic in the country.

title: Winchcombe: Capital of Mercia
summary: In Saxon times, Winchcombe was the capital of its own shire and home to a great abbey. Today, traces of this ancient importance survive in its street plan.
body: Winchcombe was once the capital of the Anglo-Saxon kingdom of Winchcombeshire, with a powerful Benedictine abbey founded in 798 AD. The abbey was demolished during the Dissolution of the Monasteries under Henry VIII, but its stones were reused in buildings throughout the town. Nearby Sudeley Castle was the home and burial place of Katherine Parr, the last wife of Henry VIII. The town retains its medieval street plan and is celebrated for its walking festival each May.

title: Cleeve Common: Wild Hilltop
summary: Cleeve Common is the largest area of unimproved limestone grassland in Gloucestershire, home to rare orchids, skylarks, and ancient earthworks.
body: At approximately 1,000 acres, Cleeve Common is the largest area of common land in Gloucestershire and a Site of Special Scientific Interest. The unimproved limestone grassland supports rare wildflowers including bee orchids, pyramidal orchids, and frog orchids. Skylarks nest on the open hillside and buzzards ride the thermals along the escarpment edge. The hilltop also contains Bronze Age ring cairns and an Iron Age cross-dyke. At 330 metres, this is the highest point on the entire Cotswold Way.

title: The Devil's Chimney
summary: This natural limestone pillar was left standing after quarrying. Legend says the devil rises from it on dark nights to sit on the hilltop.
body: The Devil's Chimney is a limestone pillar standing on Leckhampton Hill, created as a remnant of the extensive quarrying that took place from the 18th to early 20th century. The quarries supplied stone for much of Regency Cheltenham below. Local legend says the devil emerges from the chimney on dark nights to sit on the hilltop. In reality, the pillar has been reinforced with concrete and iron rods to prevent collapse. It has been climbed many times, most famously by a group of students in the 1980s who placed a traffic cone on top.

title: The 99 Yew Trees of Painswick
summary: Legend holds that there can never be more than 99 yew trees in Painswick churchyard — the devil always kills the hundredth.
body: St Mary's churchyard in Painswick is famous for its 99 clipped yew trees, many of which were planted in the 18th century. Local legend insists that there can never be 100 trees — whenever a hundredth is planted, the devil kills it. The reality is less dramatic: the count varies depending on how you define a separate tree. The churchyard also contains remarkable 17th and 18th-century table tombs carved by local masons, considered among the finest in England. The annual Clipping Ceremony (from 'ycleping', meaning embracing) sees the church encircled by parishioners holding hands.

title: Standish Wood: Ancient Woodland
summary: This ancient woodland has been continuously wooded since at least the medieval period. In spring, the forest floor erupts in a carpet of bluebells.
body: Standish Wood is classified as ancient semi-natural woodland, meaning it has been continuously wooded since at least 1600 AD and likely much longer. Dominated by beech trees, it sits on the Cotswold escarpment with dramatic views west. In late April and May, the woodland floor is carpeted with bluebells — one of the finest displays in Gloucestershire. The wood is also home to fallow deer, badgers, and a variety of woodland birds including nuthatches, treecreepers, and great spotted woodpeckers.

title: Nympsfield Long Barrow
summary: Near Coaley Peak lies Nympsfield Long Barrow, a Neolithic burial chamber dating from around 2800 BC — older than Stonehenge.
body: Just a short walk from Coaley Peak viewpoint lies Nympsfield Long Barrow, a Neolithic chambered tomb dating from around 2800 BC. When excavated, it contained the remains of around 23 individuals along with pottery and animal bones. The tomb was originally covered by a mound of earth and stone nearly 30 metres long. Today the stone chambers are exposed and freely accessible. Nearby Hetty Pegler's Tump, another long barrow, can also be explored with a torch. These monuments predate Stonehenge and the Egyptian pyramids.

title: Bath Abbey: Journey's End
summary: Bath Abbey has stood on this site since the 7th century. The current building, begun in 1499, is the last great medieval church built in England.
body: Bath Abbey, where many Cotswold Way walkers celebrate completing their 102-mile journey, has been a place of worship since the 7th century. The present building was begun in 1499 by Bishop Oliver King, who had a dream of angels ascending and descending a ladder to heaven — depicted on the west front. It is the last great medieval church built in England. The Abbey sits above the ancient Roman Baths, the only naturally occurring hot springs in the British Isles — rising at 46°C, well above body temperature. (Other thermal springs exist at Buxton and Taff's Well, but at much lower temperatures.) The waters rise from a depth of over 2,700 metres.

title: The Cotswold Lion Sheep
summary: The 'Cotswold Lion' is a rare breed of large, shaggy-fleeced sheep that once made the Cotswolds the richest region in medieval England.
body: The Cotswold Lion is a distinctive breed of sheep with long, curly fleeces that can grow to 15 inches. In the Middle Ages, their high-quality wool was so valuable it was known as the 'Golden Fleece' and was exported across Europe. The breed nearly died out in the 20th century but has been revived by dedicated farmers. You may still spot these large, gentle sheep with their characteristic fringes in fields along the Cotswold Way. The breed's historical importance is reflected in the Lord Chancellor's Woolsack — the large wool-stuffed seat in the House of Lords, a tradition dating back to the 14th century when England's wealth depended on the wool trade.

title: Peregrine Falcons at Broadway Tower
summary: Peregrine falcons nest on Broadway Tower's stonework, diving at speeds over 200mph to hunt pigeons across the hilltop.
body: Broadway Tower is home to nesting peregrine falcons, the fastest animals on Earth. During their hunting stoop (dive), they can reach speeds exceeding 200mph. The falcons were driven to near-extinction in Britain by pesticide use in the 1960s but have made a remarkable recovery. The tower's elevated position and abundant prey (mainly pigeons and starlings) make it an ideal nesting site. Birdwatchers gather at the tower in spring to watch the adults bringing food to their chicks. If you look up as you pass, you may see one circling overhead.

title: The Golden Stone: Cotswold Limestone
summary: The honey-coloured buildings of the Cotswolds are built from Great Oolite limestone, formed 170 million years ago on a tropical seabed.
body: The distinctive golden stone of the Cotswolds is Great Oolite limestone, formed around 170 million years ago during the Jurassic period. At that time, this area lay beneath a warm, shallow sea near the equator. The limestone is made up of tiny spherical grains called ooliths (from the Greek for 'egg stone'), each formed as layers of calcium carbonate built up around a grain of sand. The stone varies in colour from pale cream in the north (around Broadway) to deeper honey and amber tones in the south (near Bath). This gradual colour change is visible as you walk the Cotswold Way.

title: The Ghostly Monks of Stanway
summary: Local legend tells of hooded monks seen walking through Stanway's grounds at dusk, remnants of the medieval Tewkesbury Abbey estate.
body: Stanway House was originally part of the estate of Tewkesbury Abbey, and monks are said to have farmed this land for centuries before the Dissolution. Locals report seeing hooded figures walking silently through the grounds at dusk, particularly near the tithe barn and the old gatehouse. The barn itself dates from the 14th century and would have stored the abbey's share of the local harvest. Whether the sightings are tricks of the fading Cotswold light or something more, the atmosphere at Stanway in the evening is undeniably atmospheric.

title: Katherine Parr at Sudeley Castle
summary: Sudeley Castle near Winchcombe was the final home of Katherine Parr, the last wife of Henry VIII, who is buried in the chapel.
body: After Henry VIII died in 1547, Katherine Parr married Thomas Seymour and moved to Sudeley Castle. She died there in 1548, just days after giving birth to her daughter Mary. She was buried in the castle chapel, but her tomb was lost for centuries after the castle was damaged in the Civil War. In 1782, her coffin was accidentally rediscovered by a local man. The castle has been beautifully restored and Katherine's tomb can still be visited in St Mary's Church within the grounds, making it the only private dwelling in England where a queen is buried.

title: Walking the Edge: The Cotswold Escarpment
summary: The dramatic west-facing escarpment you walk along was created by millions of years of erosion, exposing the limestone beneath.
body: The Cotswold escarpment is the dramatic western edge of the Cotswold Hills, running roughly north-south for over 50 miles. It was formed as rivers and ice eroded the softer clay beneath the harder limestone cap, creating the steep west-facing slopes that give the Cotswold Way its most spectacular views. The escarpment rises to over 300 metres at Cleeve Hill and offers views across the Severn Vale to the Forest of Dean, the Malvern Hills, and on clear days, the mountains of Wales. Much of the Cotswold Way follows this edge, which is why the views are consistently to the west.

title: The Quarrymen of Leckhampton
summary: Leckhampton Hill was heavily quarried for 200 years, providing the stone that built Regency Cheltenham's elegant terraces.
body: From the late 1700s to the early 1900s, Leckhampton Hill was the main source of building stone for the rapidly growing spa town of Cheltenham below. The quarrymen cut deep into the hillside, leaving dramatic cliff faces that are now popular with rock climbers. An inclined tramway once carried stone blocks down to the town. Working conditions were dangerous — several quarrymen were killed in rockfalls. The Devil's Chimney pillar was left standing as the quarry was cut around it, either deliberately as a landmark or simply because the stone was too fractured to use.

title: Painswick Rococo Garden
summary: Hidden behind the village is England's only surviving complete Rococo garden, a whimsical 18th-century landscape of paths, follies, and snowdrops.
body: The Painswick Rococo Garden is a unique survivor from the 1740s, a period when garden design was moving away from formal symmetry towards more playful, naturalistic styles. The garden was created for Benjamin Hyett and features winding paths, Gothic follies, a maze, and dramatic views down a steep coombe. It fell into neglect for over a century before being rediscovered and restored from the 1980s using a painting by Thomas Robins as the guide. In February, the garden is famous for its display of over a million snowdrops, drawing visitors from across the country.

title: Bluebell Season in the Woods
summary: In late April and May, the ancient beech woods along the escarpment erupt in a carpet of native bluebells — a sight unique to Britain.
body: Britain is home to around half the world's population of native bluebells, and the ancient woodlands along the Cotswold Way are among the best places to see them. The display peaks in late April to mid-May, when the forest floor turns a vivid violet-blue. Native bluebells (Hyacinthoides non-scripta) have narrow, tubular bells that droop to one side and a sweet scent. They thrive in ancient woodland because they flower before the tree canopy closes, using the brief window of spring light. Standish Wood, Buckholt Wood, and the beech hangers above Painswick are particularly spectacular.

title: Hetty Pegler's Tump: A Name from the Past
summary: This Neolithic burial chamber near Coaley Peak is named after a 17th-century landowner — but the tomb itself is nearly 5,000 years old.
body: Hetty Pegler's Tump (formally Uley Long Barrow) is a Neolithic chambered tomb dating from around 3000 BC. It takes its popular name from Hester Pegler, who owned the surrounding land in the 1600s. The mound is 37 metres long and still has its original dry-stone walled entrance passage and burial chambers. When excavated, remains of at least 28 individuals were found inside. Unlike nearby Nympsfield Long Barrow, Hetty Pegler's Tump retains its earth covering and can be entered with a torch — a genuinely atmospheric experience. A key is available from the nearby farm.

title: Dursley and the Cloth Trade
summary: Dursley was once a thriving centre of the Cotswold cloth trade. The town's prosperity built the fine church of St James.
body: From the 14th to the 18th century, Dursley was an important centre of the Cotswold cloth trade, processing the wool from the sheep that grazed the surrounding hills. The town's wealth funded the construction of St James the Great church, with its distinctive Gothic tower. The cloth mills were powered by the streams running off the escarpment. As the Industrial Revolution moved textile production to the north of England, Dursley's cloth trade declined. The Lister family later brought engineering industry to the town — R.A. Lister & Company manufactured diesel engines and agricultural equipment here for over a century.

title: The Dursley Lantern
summary: A local legend tells of a mysterious light seen floating above the town on misty nights, said to be the ghost of a lost traveller.
body: The Dursley Lantern is a piece of local folklore describing a ghostly light that appears above the town on foggy autumn nights. The story goes that a medieval traveller, lost in the mist on the escarpment above Dursley, lit a lantern to find his way but walked over the cliff edge. His light is said to still be seen on misty nights, floating along the hilltop. While the phenomenon is likely caused by atmospheric conditions and distant lights refracting in the fog, the story has been told in Dursley pubs for generations. Some say if you see it, you should not follow.

title: The Electric Picture House
summary: Wotton-under-Edge is home to one of the oldest cinemas in the world, converted from a former inn and showing films since 1913.
body: The Electric Picture House in Wotton-under-Edge opened in 1913 and is one of the oldest cinemas still operating anywhere in the world. The building was converted from the upstairs Banqueting Room of the former Crown Inn (de-licensed in 1911), with films shown in the building as early as 1902. Though the cinema closed temporarily in 1960, 1965, and 2002, it was revived each time by the local community and reopened most recently in 2005. It retains much of its original character, seats just 100 people, and is run entirely by volunteers. The cinema shows a mix of new releases, classic films, and live event screenings. It is a remarkable survival in an age of multiplexes and streaming.

title: Edward Jenner: Pioneer of Vaccination
summary: The world's first vaccine was developed just miles from here by Edward Jenner, who grew up in the countryside around Berkeley.
body: Edward Jenner, born in Berkeley in 1749, developed the world's first successful vaccine. Working as a country doctor, he noticed that milkmaids who had caught cowpox seemed immune to smallpox. In 1796, he tested his theory by inoculating a boy with cowpox material and later exposing him to smallpox — the boy was immune. Jenner's discovery led to the eventual eradication of smallpox in 1980, saving countless millions of lives. His home in Berkeley is now a museum. The rolling countryside you walk through between Wotton and Dursley would have been familiar to Jenner on his rounds.

title: The Battle of Nibley Green
summary: In 1470, the last private battle in England was fought near here between two feuding noble families, the Talbots and the Berkeleys.
body: On 20 March 1470, Lord Lisle and Viscount Berkeley settled their long-running land dispute the medieval way — with a pitched battle. Around 1,000 men fought at Nibley Green, between Wotton-under-Edge and North Nibley. Lord Lisle was killed, shot through the face with an arrow, and his body was stripped and left on the field. The battle is considered the last private battle fought in England. A monument on the hillside above North Nibley, the Tyndale Monument, commemorates not the battle but William Tyndale, who translated the Bible into English and is believed to have been born nearby.

title: Old Sodbury Iron Age Hillfort
summary: The earthwork banks around Old Sodbury church are the remains of an Iron Age hillfort, occupied for over a thousand years.
body: The ridge at Old Sodbury has been a defensive position since at least the Iron Age, around 500 BC. The hillfort's earth ramparts and ditches enclosed a large area that would have housed a community and their livestock. The site was strategically important, controlling the route along the escarpment and the crossing of the Bristol Avon below. The Norman church of St John the Baptist was later built within the fort's defences, reusing the ancient earthworks. From the churchyard, you can still trace the line of the ramparts and appreciate why this hilltop was chosen — the views in every direction are commanding.

title: The Battle of Lansdown: Civil War on the Ridge
summary: In 1643, one of the bloodiest battles of the English Civil War was fought on the ridge above Cold Ashton.
body: On 5 July 1643, Royalist forces under Sir Ralph Hopton attacked the Parliamentary army of Sir William Waller on Lansdown Hill, just north of Cold Ashton. The battle was fierce and indecisive — both sides suffered heavy casualties in the steep terrain. Sir Bevil Grenville, one of the Royalist commanders, was killed leading a charge up the hill. A monument to Grenville still stands on the battlefield. The Royalists held the hill at nightfall but were too weakened to press their advantage. You pass near the battlefield site as you walk the final ridge towards Bath.

title: The Ghost of Hanging Hill
summary: Hanging Hill above Cold Ashton is said to be haunted by the ghost of a woman in white, seen at dawn walking towards the cliff edge.
body: Hanging Hill, which the Cotswold Way crosses between Cold Ashton and Bath, takes its ominous name from its steep, overhanging cliff. Local stories tell of a woman in white seen walking the hilltop path at dawn, always moving towards the cliff edge before vanishing. The identity of the ghost varies with the telling — some say she was a jilted bride, others a Civil War widow searching for her husband after the Battle of Lansdown. The hilltop is genuinely atmospheric in early morning mist, and the views from the edge are spectacular but vertiginous. The name 'Hanging Hill' likely comes from the Old English for 'steep slope' rather than anything more sinister.

title: The Hot Springs: 10,000 Years of Bathing
summary: Bath's hot springs have drawn people for thousands of years. The water falls as rain on the Mendip Hills and rises at 46°C after travelling over 2,700 metres underground.
body: The thermal springs at Bath are the only naturally occurring hot springs in the British Isles. Rainwater falling on the nearby Mendip Hills sinks deep underground, where geothermal energy heats it to between 64°C and 96°C. It then rises through faults in the limestone to emerge at the surface at around 46°C. The springs produce over a million litres of water per day. Archaeological finds suggest people were drawn to these springs thousands of years before the Romans arrived. The Romans built an elaborate bathing complex around the springs in the 1st century AD, and the Georgian city we see today was built on the same tradition of 'taking the waters'.

title: Robert Dover's Olimpick Games
summary: Since 1612, this hilltop has hosted England's answer to the Olympics — complete with shin-kicking, cudgel-fighting, and coursing.
body: In 1612, local lawyer Robert Dover organised the first Cotswold Olimpick Games on this natural amphitheatre above Chipping Campden. Dover, who had moved to nearby Saintbury in 1611, wanted to channel the competitive spirit of local people into organised sport. With backing from wealthy merchant Baptist Hicks and royal patronage from James I — who even lent Dover a suit of his old clothes to wear — the games became a grand affair. Events included shin-kicking, wrestling, cudgel-fighting, horse racing, and coursing with hounds. Poets Ben Jonson and Michael Drayton wrote verses in support, and the British Olympic Association has recognised the games as 'the first stirrings of Britain's Olympic beginnings.' Dover presided over the games for forty years until his death in 1652. They were revived for the 1951 Festival of Britain, again in 1963 by the Scuttlebrook Wake Committee, and have been held annually since 1966, on the Friday after Spring Bank Holiday. The shin-kicking championship remains the headline event — competitors stuff straw down their trouser legs for padding and try to kick each other's shins until one falls. The word 'stickler' — meaning someone strict about rules — is believed to originate from the referee at these games, who used a stick to separate fighters.

title: The View from Dover's Hill
summary: On a clear day, you can see the Malvern Hills, the Black Mountains of Wales, and the glint of the River Avon winding through the Vale of Evesham.
body: Dover's Hill sits at 230 metres (754 feet) on the northern tip of the Cotswold escarpment, offering one of the finest viewpoints in the region. The panorama extends across the Vale of Evesham to the Malvern Hills, 25 miles to the west, and on exceptionally clear days you can make out the Black Mountains of Wales beyond. Below, the flat farmland of the vale — once a vast lake formed by glacial meltwater — produces some of England's finest asparagus and plums. The site was saved for public access in 1926 when Campden artist Frederick Landseer Griggs bought the hill at auction to prevent it falling into private hands. He went into debt for several years before the money was repaid and the land was given to the National Trust. The hill is a Site of Special Scientific Interest, with unimproved limestone grassland supporting cowslips, rock-rose, and pyramidal orchids. Skylarks nest here in summer, and you may hear their song as you walk the first mile of the Cotswold Way.

title: The Coaching Road over Fish Hill
summary: In the 1700s, Broadway was a bustling stagecoach stop where horses were changed for the gruelling climb up Fish Hill.
body: The coaching route over Fish Hill opened in the 1500s, connecting Worcester to Oxford and London via the A44. The steep climb up the escarpment was so demanding that Broadway, at the foot of the hill, became a thriving stagecoach stop. Horses were rested and changed, passengers refreshed, and grooms prepared fresh teams for the ascent. At the height of the coaching era, Broadway had 33 public houses — compared to just three today. The Lygon Arms, still standing on the village green, hosted both King Charles I and Oliver Cromwell at different times during the Civil War. The name 'Fish Hill' is something of a mystery — there is no water, let alone fish, anywhere near the summit. Some historians believe it derives from an old English word for a ridge or escarpment edge. The hill was also the site of a quarry, though you would struggle to guess it today — nature has long since reclaimed the workings. The picnic area near the summit, managed by Worcestershire County Council, sits in the old quarry and is surrounded by wildflowers and mature beech trees.

title: Giants Steps and Wild Orchids
summary: Fish Hill's old quarry has been transformed into a wildflower meadow with stepped terraces known as the Giant's Steps.
body: Fish Hill Picnic Place occupies the site of an old quarry on the Cotswold escarpment. Over decades, nature has softened the quarry's angular cuts into gentle, stepped terraces known locally as the Giant's Steps. These south-facing limestone ledges have become a haven for wildflowers: pyramidal orchids, bee orchids, wild thyme, and marjoram flourish in the thin, chalky soil. Common blue and chalkhill blue butterflies feed on the horseshoe vetch that grows on the steeper slopes. The site is managed as a nature reserve, with careful grazing by sheep keeping the grassland open and preventing scrub from taking over. In autumn, the surrounding beech woods turn a spectacular gold — Fish Hill is one of the best places on the northern Cotswolds to see the autumn colour change. From the picnic area, you can see Broadway Tower standing on the adjacent hilltop, just a mile along the escarpment. The contrast between the wild quarry landscape and the manicured tower grounds captures two very different sides of the Cotswolds.

title: Charles Wade's Cabinet of Curiosities
summary: Just down the hill from here, a Tudor manor houses 22,000 objects collected by one eccentric man — who slept in the shed so his treasures could have the house.
body: In 1919, architect Charles Paget Wade bought Snowshill Manor, a 16th-century house tucked into the hillside below the trail. Over the next three decades, he filled its 20 rooms with over 22,000 objects — Japanese samurai armour, bicycles, toys, musical instruments, clocks, and tools. His motto was 'Let nothing perish.' What makes Snowshill truly eccentric is that Wade never lived in the house. He slept in a small cottage next door, leaving every room for his collection. He arranged objects by theme rather than period, creating immersive rooms with names like 'Zenith' and 'Admiral.' Wade's childhood fascination with collecting began at age seven, when his grandmother in Great Yarmouth allowed him to open her lacquered cabinet of curiosities every Sunday. He trained as an architect and designed houses in Hampstead Garden Suburb before inheriting enough money to pursue collecting full-time. Virginia Woolf and J.B. Priestley visited. In 1951, Wade donated the manor to the National Trust on the condition it remain open to the public. It is one of the most unusual properties the Trust manages — 95 per cent of Wade's oriental and Italian treasures were found in England.

title: Lavender on the Ridge
summary: The ridge above Snowshill was once planted with lavender fields. Today, the trail crosses open farmland with wide views across the Cotswold valleys.
body: The ridge above Snowshill sits at around 250 metres, offering panoramic views across the rolling Cotswold valleys towards Winchcombe and Sudeley Castle. The village of Snowshill itself — its name derives from the Old English 'Snawesille,' recorded in the Domesday Book of 1086 — sits in a sheltered hollow below the ridge. The area was given to Winchcombe Abbey by King Coenwulf of Mercia in 821 AD and remained monastic property until the Dissolution. The thin limestone soil and south-facing slopes once made this area ideal for lavender cultivation. While commercial lavender farming has largely moved elsewhere, the dry, chalky grassland still supports wild marjoram and thyme — plants from the same aromatic family. Walking the ridge in high summer, you may catch their scent on the breeze. The crossroads near this marker is where the Cotswold Way intersects an old drove road that linked Snowshill to the markets at Stow-on-the-Wold. Drovers would have walked their sheep along this route for centuries, and the wide grass verges are a relic of the space needed to move large flocks.

title: The Holy Blood of Hailes
summary: For 270 years, pilgrims flocked to this abbey to venerate a phial said to contain the blood of Christ. Henry VIII's commissioners declared it nothing more than honey coloured with saffron.
body: Hailes Abbey was founded in 1246 by Richard, Earl of Cornwall, brother of King Henry III, after he survived a shipwreck and vowed to build a monastery in thanksgiving. Twenty monks and ten lay brothers arrived from Beaulieu Abbey in Hampshire to establish the community. The abbey was dedicated on 5 November 1251 in the presence of the King himself and thirteen bishops. But it was Richard's son Edmund who transformed Hailes from a modest monastery into one of England's greatest pilgrimage sites. In 1270, Edmund brought back from the Crusades a phial said to contain the blood of Christ, authenticated by the Patriarch of Jerusalem. A magnificent new east end was built to house the relic, modelled on Henry III's rebuilding of Westminster Abbey. For nearly 270 years, pilgrims travelled from across England to venerate the Holy Blood. Geoffrey Chaucer referenced it in The Canterbury Tales. Then came Henry VIII's Reformation. In 1538, commissioners seized the phial and declared its contents to be 'honey clarified and coloured with saffron.' The Abbot, Stephen Sagar, hoping to save the abbey, admitted the relic was a fake. It did not work. On Christmas Eve 1539, Sagar and his 21 monks signed the surrender. The abbey was stripped and the buildings left to decay.

title: Reading the Ruins
summary: The cloister arches and floor tiles that survive at Hailes tell the story of a wealthy medieval community that housed over 30 monks.
body: Walking among the ruins of Hailes Abbey today, you can still trace the layout of a complete Cistercian monastery. The cloister foundations show where monks walked in contemplation, the refectory where they ate in silence while scripture was read aloud, and the chapter house where daily business was conducted. Excavations have uncovered beautifully decorated floor tiles, now displayed in the on-site museum. The tiles feature heraldic designs, mythical beasts, and intricate geometric patterns — evidence of the abbey's wealth during its pilgrimage heyday. The Cistercians were known for choosing remote, well-watered valleys for their monasteries, and Hailes fits the pattern perfectly. The site sits in a quiet valley with a stream running through it, surrounded by the same gentle hills the monks would have known. The abbey church was originally cruciform (cross-shaped), but the addition of the Holy Blood chapel in the 1270s created an unusual eastern extension with a ring of radiating chapels — a design borrowed from French Gothic cathedrals. Only the foundation walls survive, but enough remains to appreciate the scale of what was once here. The site is owned by the National Trust but managed by English Heritage.

title: The False Door of Belas Knap
summary: This 5,500-year-old tomb was built with a fake entrance to protect the dead. The real burial chambers are hidden along the sides.
body: Belas Knap is one of the finest Neolithic long barrows in England, dating to around 3700 BC — older than Stonehenge and the Great Pyramids. The barrow stretches 54 metres long, 18 metres wide, and nearly 4 metres high, built from local Cotswold limestone. Its most striking feature is the false entrance at the northern end: an elaborate doorway of upright stones that leads nowhere. The real burial chambers are concealed in the sides of the mound, invisible from the outside when originally sealed. Why the deception? Some archaeologists believe the false entrance was intended to deter tomb robbers. Others suggest it served as a 'spirit door' — a portal for the dead to pass between worlds. The name Belas Knap comes from the Old English 'bel' (beacon) and 'cnaepp' (hilltop). When excavated in 1863, the remains of 31 people were found across four chambers. Behind the false entrance, five children aged between six months and eight years were discovered, along with the skull of a young man. The round shape of this skull — unusual among the typically long-skulled Neolithic people — led Victorian archaeologists to speculate he was a captured enemy, perhaps sacrificed. Romano-British pottery found inside one chamber suggests the tomb was still being entered nearly 4,000 years after it was built.

title: The Hilltop Builders
summary: The people who built Belas Knap chose this exposed hilltop deliberately — it commands views across two valleys and would have been visible for miles.
body: Belas Knap sits at 305 metres on a windswept hilltop above Winchcombe, commanding views across the valleys below. The Neolithic people who built it around 3700 BC chose this position deliberately. The barrow was meant to be seen — a monument to the community's ancestors, visible from the settlements in the valleys on either side. Building it was a significant undertaking for a Stone Age community. The thousands of limestone slabs were quarried locally and dragged uphill, then carefully stacked using dry-stone walling techniques remarkably similar to those still used by Cotswold wallers today. The four burial chambers were constructed first, then the entire structure was buried under a mound of earth and rubble. The barrow belongs to the Cotswold-Severn group — a style of tomb found scattered along the Severn valley, all sharing a similar trapezoid shape. After excavation in the 1860s and further work in the 1928-1931, the mound was restored to its original contour. Today it looks much as it would have 5,500 years ago. Flint tools and arrowheads found nearby are displayed at Winchcombe Museum — notable because flint does not occur naturally in Gloucestershire, proving these early communities traded with distant regions.

title: The Queen Who Rests at Sudeley
summary: Sudeley Castle is the only private dwelling in England where a queen is buried — Katherine Parr, the last wife of Henry VIII.
body: After Henry VIII died in January 1547, his sixth and surviving wife Katherine Parr married Thomas Seymour and moved to Sudeley Castle. It was here, on 30 August 1548, that she gave birth to her daughter Mary. Five days later, on 5 September, Katherine died of what was described as childbed fever. She was 36 years old. Lady Jane Grey, then aged about 11 and living at Sudeley as Seymour's ward, served as chief mourner at what became the first Protestant royal funeral in English history. Katherine was buried in St Mary's Church within the castle grounds. Over the following two centuries, her tomb was vandalised and its location forgotten entirely. In 1782, a group of local men accidentally uncovered her lead-lined coffin while digging. They opened it and reportedly found her body remarkably well preserved. The coffin was reburied, lost, and found again in 1817 before finally being given a permanent marble tomb in 1863. Today, Sudeley remains the only private castle in England where a queen is buried. The castle has 10th-century origins — the first castle was built around 1139, though the current structures are mostly 15th century — and has hosted Henry VIII, Anne Boleyn, Richard III, and Elizabeth I. It was badly damaged during the Civil War and lay ruinous for nearly 200 years before the Dent family began its restoration in 1837.

title: Ten Centuries of Sudeley Gardens
summary: The Queen's Garden at Sudeley is planted as a Tudor parterre, recreating the style Katherine Parr would have walked through in 1548.
body: Sudeley's gardens span ten centuries of design. The Queen's Garden, surrounding the ruined tithe barn, is laid out as a Tudor parterre with low box hedges, roses, and herbs — a recreation of the style Katherine Parr would have known during her brief time here. The garden was redesigned in the 1850s by Emma Dent, who also commissioned the spectacular yew walk that frames the approach to the castle. In spring, the White Garden fills with snowdrops, followed by tulips and then the famous old roses that climb the castle walls. The Knot Garden, near the entrance, uses intertwined hedges of different coloured box to create traditional geometric patterns. Beyond the formal gardens, the 1,200-acre estate includes ancient parkland where fallow deer have grazed since medieval times. Pheasant's Eye daffodils, a rare wild variety, bloom in the meadows each April. The views from the garden terrace look north towards Cleeve Common and the high escarpment you have just descended from — a perspective that puts your walk in context.

title: The Manor in the Hidden Valley
summary: Postlip Hall has been lived in since the 14th century. Today it houses a cohousing community of eight families in a Grade I listed manor.
body: Tucked into a wooded valley off the road between Winchcombe and Cheltenham, Postlip Hall is a Grade I listed Cotswold manor house with origins in the 14th century. The Jacobean frontage you see from the Cotswold Way was added around 1614 — a date carved into one of the fireplaces inside. The hall contains a room known as the Cromwellian Room, with fine 17th-century oak panelling that may originally have served as a chapel. A separate Norman chapel dating from 1145 stands in the grounds, along with a medieval tithe barn now used for concerts and weddings. In 1969, a group of pioneers bought the hall and established what became the Postlip Community — one of Britain's earliest cohousing experiments. Today, eight families live in separate units within the manor, sharing the grounds, the tithe barn, and a communal spirit. They host an annual Cotswold Beer Festival and folk music weekends in the barn. The hall marks the transition from the gentle Winchcombe valley to the exposed upland of Cleeve Common. From here, the Cotswold Way climbs steadily for two miles to reach the highest point on the entire trail at 330 metres.

title: The Norman Chapel at Postlip
summary: Hidden in the grounds of Postlip Hall is a tiny Norman chapel dating from 1145 — one of the oldest intact buildings on the Cotswold Way.
body: The Chapel of St James at Postlip is a small Norman building dating from around 1145, making it nearly 900 years old and one of the oldest intact structures you will encounter on the Cotswold Way. It measures just 12 metres long and 5 metres wide, with thick rubblestone walls and a simple chancel arch. The chapel was built as a private place of worship for the lords of Postlip manor, at a time when the nearest parish church was a long walk away in Winchcombe. During the English Civil War, the chapel is said to have been used as a stable by Parliamentarian troops. It fell into disuse in the 18th century but was restored in the Victorian period and again in the 1970s by the Postlip Community. Today it is used for occasional services and community gatherings. The building retains its original proportions and much of its Norman stonework, including a small lancet window in the east wall. Standing inside, you are in a space that has remained essentially unchanged for nearly nine centuries — a rare experience in a landscape where most medieval buildings have been extensively altered.

title: The Reservoir That Saved Cheltenham
summary: Built in 1886 to supply clean water to the growing spa town of Cheltenham, Dowdeswell Reservoir is now a wildlife haven and flood defence.
body: In the 1880s, Cheltenham was growing rapidly as a fashionable spa town, and its water supply could not keep up. Cheltenham Borough Council acquired the local waterworks in 1878 and between 1883 and 1886 constructed Dowdeswell Reservoir by damming the upper valley of the River Chelt. The reservoir served as the town's primary water supply for over a century. When Severn Trent Water commissioned the larger Mythe Treatment Works on the River Severn, Dowdeswell was repurposed as a flood storage reservoir — a role it plays to this day, protecting Cheltenham from flooding after heavy rain. The surrounding woodland, classified as ancient — meaning it has been continuously wooded since at least the 1600s — was established as a nature reserve in 1973. Common toads migrate to the reservoir to spawn each spring in such numbers that signs are erected on the road to warn drivers. The ancient woodland supports bluebells, wood anemone, early-purple orchids, and ramsons (wild garlic). Great crested grebes nest on the reservoir itself, and you may hear their elaborate courtship calls echoing across the water. The Cotswold Way runs along the western edge of the wood, and the dam can be glimpsed through the trees.

title: The Other Source of the Thames
summary: A Latin plaque here claims these springs are the true source of the River Thames. The debate has lasted over 500 years.
body: A stone plaque beside the springs reads "Hic tuus o Tamesine Pater septemgeminus fons" — "Here, O Father Thames, is your sevenfold spring." The claim is serious: the River Churn, which rises here, flows south through Cirencester before joining the Thames near Cricklade. If you measure from Seven Springs to the sea, the Thames is 229 miles long — 9 miles longer than the River Severn, making it the longest river in the United Kingdom. The official source at Thames Head near Kemble is both lower (110 metres versus 215 metres) and seasonal — those springs often run dry in summer. Seven Springs flows year-round. The debate has raged since at least 1546, when the scholar John Leland declared Thames Head as the source. It even reached Parliament, with MPs from rival constituencies arguing their case. In 2012, Coberley Parish Council diplomatically posted a notice stating that Seven Springs is "certainly one of the sources" and "held by many to be the ultimate source." Standing here, with water reliably bubbling from the ground at the head of a valley that eventually reaches the heart of London, you may form your own view.

title: Spring-Fed Habitats
summary: The constant flow of spring water here creates a unique microhabitat — cool, lime-rich water supports rare mosses and invertebrates.
body: The seven springs that give this hamlet its name emerge from the junction of the Great Oolite limestone and the underlying Fuller's Earth clay. Rainwater falling on the Cotswold plateau above percolates down through the porous limestone until it hits the impervious clay layer, where it flows laterally and emerges at the surface as springs. This geological mechanism is the same one that creates springs along the entire Cotswold escarpment — but here the flow is unusually reliable, thanks to the large catchment area of the plateau above. The constant-temperature, lime-rich water creates a microhabitat that supports species rare elsewhere: water-cress, golden saxifrage, and several species of liverwort and moss thrive in the permanently damp conditions around the spring head. Freshwater shrimp and caddisfly larvae inhabit the infant River Churn as it begins its 39-mile journey to the Thames. The A436 road junction here has been an important crossing point since Roman times — the Ermin Way, the Roman road from Gloucester to Cirencester, passes just to the south. The springs would have been a natural watering point for travellers and their horses.

title: Five Thousand Years on Crickley Hill
summary: This hilltop has been fortified since 4000 BC. Over 400 flint arrowheads found at the entrance tell of a violent Neolithic attack.
body: Crickley Hill has been occupied and fought over for at least 5,000 years. The earliest fortification, dating to around 4000 BC, was a Neolithic causewayed enclosure — a ritual and ceremonial gathering place surrounded by ditches and banks. Around 2500 BC, this settlement was attacked and burned. Archaeologists found over 400 flint arrowheads concentrated at the entrance, evidence of a sustained assault. After two millennia of abandonment, an Iron Age community built a new hillfort here between 700 and 500 BC. The first phase featured rectangular houses — unusual for the period — arranged in a planned layout, suggesting a high degree of social organisation. The second phase replaced these with round houses, including one large building interpreted as a chief's hall. This settlement too was violently destroyed, with evidence of burning at the entrance gateway. Summer excavations between 1969 and 1993 uncovered this remarkable sequence of occupation. The hill is now jointly managed by the National Trust and Gloucestershire Wildlife Trust. Over 1,300 species have been recorded on the site, including chalk hill blue butterflies and rare orchids. The poet Ivor Gurney immortalised the hill in verse, recalling how mention of Crickley bonded Gloucestershire soldiers in the trenches of the Western Front.

title: The Birdlip Mirror: An Iron Age Masterpiece
summary: In 1879, quarrymen discovered a 2,000-year-old bronze mirror buried with a woman of high status. Her identity remains a mystery.
body: In 1879, quarryman Joseph Barnfield was digging for stone at Barrow Wake when he uncovered a burial that would become one of the most significant Iron Age finds in Britain. Alongside the skeleton of a woman in her mid-thirties lay a bronze mirror of extraordinary craftsmanship, a silver-gilt brooch, an amber necklace, and two bronze bowls. The mirror, now known as the Birdlip Mirror, dates to around 50 BC. Its kidney-shaped plate is highly polished on one side to achieve reflectivity, while the reverse bears intricate engraved curvilinear patterns in the La Tène Celtic art style — swirling, interconnected designs that demonstrate remarkable artistic sophistication. The mirror is made from three pieces: the plate, a cast handle, and a tubular binding strip around the edge. Because it was buried shortly after being made, it survives in exceptional condition. Who was this woman? Two men were buried alongside her, and the quality of her grave goods suggests high status — perhaps a tribal leader or priestess. The most fanciful theory identifies her as Boudica, though there is no evidence for this. A facial reconstruction created by the Museum of Gloucester offers a glimpse of how she may have looked. The mirror and grave goods are now displayed at the Museum of Gloucester.

title: The View from Barrow Wake
summary: On a clear day, the panorama from Barrow Wake stretches from the Malvern Hills to the Forest of Dean, with Gloucester Cathedral glinting below.
body: Barrow Wake is one of the finest viewpoints on the Cotswold Way, offering a panorama that few other spots can match. Looking west from the escarpment edge, the land drops away dramatically to the Severn Vale — a flat, green plain that was once a vast post-glacial lake. Directly below lies the city of Gloucester, its cathedral tower visible as a pale stone finger rising above the rooftops. Beyond the city, the River Severn winds through its floodplain, reflecting silver when the light is right. To the north-west, the Malvern Hills form a distinctive ridge on the horizon, 30 miles away. To the west, the Forest of Dean fills the view, and on the clearest days you can make out the Brecon Beacons of Wales beyond. The viewpoint sits on a biological and geological Site of Special Scientific Interest. The unimproved limestone grassland supports over 100 species of wildflower, including pyramidal orchids and horseshoe vetch. Twenty-two species of butterfly have been recorded here, including the nationally rare chalk hill blue and Duke of Burgundy. The "barrow" in Barrow Wake refers to the burial mound where the Birdlip Mirror was discovered in 1879 — you are standing on ground that has been significant to human communities for over 2,000 years.

title: Birdlip: Gateway to the Escarpment
summary: The steep Birdlip Hill has been a key route between the Cotswolds and Gloucester since Roman times, when the Ermin Way crossed here.
body: Birdlip sits at the point where the ancient Ermin Way — the Roman road from Gloucester (Glevum) to Cirencester (Corinium) — crosses the Cotswold escarpment. The name Birdlip may derive from the Old English "bride's leap," though the exact origin is disputed. The steep descent of Birdlip Hill, dropping over 200 metres in less than a mile, has been both a challenge and a landmark for travellers for two millennia. Before the modern A417 was built, the old road through the village was the main route between Gloucester and London. Horse-drawn coaches struggled with the gradient, and a team of extra horses was kept at the top of the hill to help vehicles reach the summit. The village pub, the Royal George Hotel, served these travellers for centuries. During both World Wars, the surrounding woodland was used for military training, and concrete structures from this period can still be found among the trees. Today Birdlip is a quiet village of around 200 people, but its position on the escarpment edge gives it a strategic significance that has been recognised since the Iron Age — the hillforts at Crickley Hill and Barrow Wake, both within walking distance, controlled this crossing point for centuries before the Romans arrived.

title: The Cheese Rolling: Centuries of Madness
summary: Every Spring Bank Holiday, competitors hurl themselves down this near-vertical hill chasing a 7 to 9-pound wheel of Double Gloucester cheese.
body: The Cooper's Hill Cheese-Rolling and Wake is one of the most extraordinary sporting events in the world. Every Spring Bank Holiday Monday, competitors launch themselves down a 200-yard slope with a gradient approaching 50 per cent, chasing a 7 to 9-pound wheel of Double Gloucester cheese. The cheese, which has a one-second head start, can reach 70 mph. No one catches it — the first person to reach the bottom wins. The event's origins are uncertain but are believed to be several hundred years old. Some historians link it to ancient pagan fertility rites — rolling objects down hills to symbolise the turning of the seasons. Others suggest it began as a way of maintaining grazing rights on the common land. Since 1988, the cheese has been supplied by Diana Smart and her son Rod from their farm in Churcham. Multiple races are held during the day, with separate events for men and women. Injuries are common and often spectacular — the local volunteer ambulance crew treats broken bones, dislocations, and concussions every year. In 2009, around 15,000 spectators turned up, three times the safe capacity, leading to the event being officially cancelled the following year. Locals ran it anyway. They have done so every year since. The most successful competitor in the event's history is Chris Anderson, who won 23 races over 15 years. In recent years, international competitors from Germany, Australia, Japan, and the United States have travelled to Gloucestershire specifically for this event.

title: The Beech Woods of Cooper's Hill
summary: The ancient beech woodland surrounding Cooper's Hill is home to wild garlic, fallow deer, and a remarkable variety of fungi.
body: Cooper's Hill is surrounded by some of the finest beech woodland on the Cotswold escarpment. The trees, many over 200 years old, form a dense canopy that filters the sunlight into a green cathedral of straight, silver-grey trunks. In spring, the woodland floor erupts with ramsons (wild garlic), whose pungent scent fills the air — you will smell it before you see it. Bluebells follow in late April, and early-purple orchids dot the clearings. The steep western slope — the same slope used for cheese rolling — is too precipitous for trees and is covered instead with unimproved limestone grassland. This combination of ancient woodland, grassland, and exposed rock creates a diverse habitat. Over 30 species of butterfly have been recorded here, including the white-letter hairstreak, which depends on the elms that survive in the hedgerows. Fallow deer, descendants of animals introduced by the Normans, move through the wood at dawn and dusk. Autumn brings a spectacular display of fungi — look for the red-capped fly agaric, the classic red-and-white toadstool of fairy tales, beneath the beeches. The Cotswold Way passes through this woodland, and the contrast between the quiet beech groves and the notorious cheese-rolling slope is striking.

title: Holst's Carol Born in Cranham
summary: Gustav Holst composed the tune for 'In the Bleak Midwinter' in Cranham, and named it after the village.
body: Every Christmas, millions sing a tune called 'Cranham' without knowing where the name comes from. In 1906, Gustav Holst — born in nearby Cheltenham in 1874 — composed the melody for Christina Rossetti's poem 'In the Bleak Midwinter' while staying in this quiet Gloucestershire village. His friend and mentor Ralph Vaughan Williams had commissioned three carols for The English Hymnal, published that October by Oxford University Press. Holst wrote the tune at a house now called Midwinter Cottage, opposite the Black Horse Inn, where his family had deep roots — his grandparents and mother had lived in the parish. Rossetti's poem, first published in 1872 in the American magazine Scribner's Monthly, is irregular in metre, and Holst's achievement was to craft a melody adaptable enough to follow its shifting rhythms while remaining simple enough for a congregation to sing. The result is one of the most enduring pieces of English church music. A rival setting by Harold Darke, composed in 1911 for a trained choir, was voted the best Christmas carol by leading choirmasters in a 2008 poll — but it is Holst's congregational version that most people know. The next time you hear 'In the Bleak Midwinter' on Christmas Eve, you are hearing the woods and valleys around this very spot, distilled into song.

title: The Ancient Beechwoods of Cranham
summary: Cranham sits within one of England's most important beech woodlands, home to over 780 species of fungi.
body: The beech woods surrounding Cranham are not merely old — they are ancient in the fullest sense. Buckholt Wood, whose name derives from the Saxon word for beechwood, is believed to have existed continuously since the original wildwood developed after the last ice age retreated roughly 10,000 years ago. Today it forms part of the Cotswold Commons and Beechwoods National Nature Reserve, the largest nature reserve in the Cotswolds at 665 hectares. The reserve was notified as a Site of Special Scientific Interest in 1954 and is protected as a European Special Area of Conservation. Beneath the beech canopy, the ground flora is unusually rich for this type of woodland. Wild strawberry carpets the drier slopes, and rarer species include bird's-nest orchid, narrow-lipped helleborine, stinking hellebore and yellow star-of-Bethlehem. But it is the fungi that truly set Buckholt Wood apart: over 780 species have been recorded here, including the extremely rare white variant of the scarlet elfcup, of which there are only a handful of records nationally. The beech trees also harbour rare spiders and snails found almost exclusively in ancient woodland, while disused limestone mines on the hillside serve as winter roosts for several bat species. Tawny owls, tree pipits and lesser-spotted woodpeckers breed in the canopy above. The Cotswold Way threads through this living museum, connecting walkers to a woodland ecosystem that has persisted, largely unbroken, since the Stone Age.

title: The Village That Bought Its Common
summary: In 2008, Cranham villagers raised £120,000 to buy their common when it came up for sale for the first time in 150 years.
body: In 2008, the residents of Cranham faced a prospect that galvanised the entire village: their common — 24 hectares of unimproved limestone grassland at the heart of the settlement — came up for sale for the first time in 150 years. Unimproved grassland, never ploughed or reseeded, is one of England's rarest habitats, and Cranham Common is a particularly fine example. It supports roughly 180 species of wildflower, including common spotted, pyramidal, fly, lesser butterfly and early purple orchids, alongside limestone specialists like milkwort, rock-rose and wild thyme. More than half of England's butterfly species have been recorded here, including the small blue and chalkhill blue. The rufous grasshopper, a scarce insect largely confined to southern chalk and limestone sites, also thrives on the common, alongside glowworms, slowworms and adders. Determined not to lose this to development or neglect, the village pulled together with support from two local trusts to raise the £120,000 purchase price. The Cranham Common Trust was created as the new custodian. The common's history as shared land stretches back centuries — by the early fourteenth century, tenants of the Brimpsfield estate already held common pasture rights over these slopes. That the village fought to preserve this tradition seven hundred years later says something about the hold this landscape has on the people who live in it.

title: Kimsbury: Three Thousand Years on the Beacon
summary: The Iron Age hillfort on Painswick Beacon is one of only about 50 large multivallate hillforts recorded in Britain.
body: The earthworks you walk through on Painswick Beacon are the remains of Kimsbury hillfort, built sometime between 400 and 100 BC. The name first appears in records from 1263-84 as Kynemaresburia — Old English for 'Cynemar's fortification' — suggesting the site was already recognised as an ancient strongpoint in the Saxon period. Covering roughly eight acres, Kimsbury is a rare example of a large multivallate hillfort, with only about 50 such sites recorded nationally. On the south side, three concentric banks enclose deep ditches, the innermost bank being the most imposing. To the north, the builders relied on the natural scarp of the Cotswold escarpment as defence enough. The fort did not fall silent after the Iron Age. Roman-era pottery, samian ware, amphorae and two coins — one Constantinian, the other earlier — have been excavated from the site, indicating continued occupation or use into at least the third century AD. During the English Civil War in 1643, Royalist forces occupied the beacon as an outpost during the siege of Gloucester. After the siege was lifted on 5 September, King Charles I himself retreated through Painswick, reportedly looking east from the hilltop and declaring 'This must be Paradise' — giving the valley and hamlet below a name they still carry. An £80,000 restoration project has since repaired erosion damage to the ancient ramparts caused by the feet of walkers and history-lovers.

title: Golf Among the Ramparts Since 1891
summary: Painswick Golf Club, founded in 1891, is one of Gloucestershire's oldest courses, laid out inside an Iron Age hillfort.
body: In 1891, David Brown — winner of the 1886 Open Championship at Musselburgh, who later moved to the Malvern Club — was charged with an unusual commission: lay out a golf course inside a 2,500-year-old hillfort. The result was Painswick Golf Club, one of the oldest courses in Gloucestershire, its fairways threading between the banks and ditches of Kimsbury Camp on the summit of Painswick Beacon. Brown's original nine-hole layout was played in the opposite direction to the present course. The early clubhouse was the Royal William pub, which served golfers until 1915, and committee meetings were held at Kimsbury House, home of Sir John Percival. The course was extended to eighteen holes in 1907. At 5,000 yards with a par of 67, it is not long by modern standards, but length is beside the point here. The course sits at over 280 metres on the Cotswold escarpment, exposed to whatever the weather sends across the Severn Vale. On a clear day, the panorama takes in the Malvern Hills to the north, the Brecon Beacons to the west, and the Forest of Dean beyond the silver thread of the Severn. Golfers share the hilltop with Iron Age earthworks, wildflowers and the walkers of the Cotswold Way, which crosses the course near the beacon's summit obelisk. It is one of the few places in England where you can lose a ball in a ditch dug three millennia ago.

title: Edge: The Village on the Brink
summary: The hamlet of Edge sits precisely where the Cotswold limestone gives way to the Severn Vale, giving it both its name and its character.
body: Edge does exactly what its name promises. This small hamlet perches on the western lip of the Cotswold escarpment, at the point where the Jurassic limestone tilts and breaks away into the Severn Vale far below. It has been part of the larger parish of Painswick since at least the medieval period, one of several outlying settlements — alongside Sheepscombe and Slad — that fell under the market town's administration. In 1873, Edge gained its own ecclesiastical parish, carved from both Painswick and Haresfield parishes, and St John the Baptist church was erected in 1866 to serve a population recorded at just 322. The village never grew large, and that is part of its appeal. It has a village hall, a green used for the annual fete, and two adjacent commons. The Edgemoor Inn, with over two hundred years of history, overlooks the Painswick Valley and has long served as a rest stop for walkers on the Cotswold Way, sitting roughly at the trail's midpoint. From the village, the land drops steeply westward through ancient woodland to the vale, while eastward the gentler dip slope rolls away towards Stroud. Edge is the kind of place that exists because of geography rather than industry — a settlement defined entirely by the geological accident that put a cliff edge here, where the limestone runs out and the view begins.

title: Rudge Hill and the Rare Butterfly
summary: The limestone grassland at Edge is one of the last strongholds of the Duke of Burgundy butterfly in Gloucestershire.
body: Just south of Edge village lies Rudge Hill Common, formerly known as Edge Common, a sweep of unimproved Jurassic limestone grassland that forms part of the Cotswold Commons and Beechwoods National Nature Reserve. The common was renamed after the reserve was created in 1974 by amalgamating ten individual sites, but most locals still call it Edge Common. Among the wildflowers and grasses of this exposed hillside lives one of Gloucestershire's rarest butterflies: the Duke of Burgundy. Despite its grand name, it is a small, inconspicuous insect with chequered brown and orange wings, and it is declining alarmingly across Britain. Rudge Hill is one of its last strongholds in the county. The butterfly depends on primrose and cowslip plants, where the females lay their eggs on the underside of leaves. Conservation work by Natural England has involved planting large areas of primula to boost the food supply, arranging light autumn cattle grazing to prevent scrub encroachment, and reducing scrub cover to no more than twenty per cent of the site. The effort appears to be working — the Duke of Burgundy is flourishing here. The common also supports chalkhill blue, Adonis blue, small blue, dingy skipper, green hairstreak, silver-washed fritillary and marbled white butterflies, making this short stretch of the Cotswold Way one of the richest for lepidoptera on the entire trail. In summer, the hillside hums.

title: Two Thousand Coins on the Hilltop
summary: In the 1830s, a hoard of 2,000 late Roman coins was discovered on Ring Hill at Haresfield, dating from AD 306-340.
body: In the 1830s, someone digging on Ring Hill at Haresfield made a remarkable find: a hoard of roughly 2,000 Roman coins, many in mint condition. The coins dated mainly from the period AD 306-340, the era of Constantine the Great, when the Roman Empire was undergoing its transformation from paganism to Christianity. For decades after the discovery, Ring Hill was interpreted as a Roman camp, and the coins seemed to confirm it. But more recent archaeological work has rewritten that story. The earthworks on Ring Hill — a promontory fort covering about five hectares at the end of a spur projecting westward from the Cotswold escarpment — are now identified as Iron Age in origin, predating the Roman coins by several centuries. The Romans evidently found the site useful too: surface scatters of pottery, roof tile and limestone slabs at the eastern end of Ring Hill point to a Romano-British building, and fragments of glass vessels and third- to fourth-century pottery have also been recorded. Only two coins from the original hoard are known to survive: one of Claudius Gothicus and one of Constantine, both now in Gloucester City Museum. A single Urbs Roma coin in mint condition, attributed to the hoard, is held by Stroud Museum. The earthworks were first mapped by Samuel Lysons in 1797 and described in detail by Lloyd Baker in 1821, who recognised them as part of a chain of defensive sites running along the Cotswold escarpment.

title: The Beacon and the Bulwarks
summary: Haresfield Beacon sits within a layered landscape of Bronze Age barrows, an Iron Age hillfort, and a WWII observation bunker.
body: Standing at the topograph on Haresfield Beacon, you are at the tip of an extraordinary V-shaped promontory jutting westward from the Cotswold escarpment, with the ground falling steeply away on three sides. The three-dimensional direction finder identifies landmarks visible on a clear day: the Malvern Hills, the Forest of Dean, the Brecon Beacons, and the silver curve of the River Severn far below. But the real depth here is underfoot. The site has been occupied and modified for at least four thousand years. Bronze Age round barrows sit on the western end of Ring Hill, marking the burials of people who lived here two millennia before the Romans arrived. East of Ring Hill, a substantial earthwork known as the Bulwarks crosses the neck of the spur — a single bank 45 feet wide and 7 feet high with an outer ditch 40 feet wide and 7 feet deep. This unexcavated hillfort is a scheduled monument. Just metres from the car park stands a brick observation bunker from the Second World War, a reminder that this commanding viewpoint has served military purposes into living memory. The National Trust acquired the estate and commissioned an archaeological survey in 1994-95 by Charles Parry of Gloucestershire County Council. The veteran beech trees in nearby Shortwood are over two hundred years old, and records show the woodland was owned by Gloucester Abbey as early as 1297. Seven centuries of recorded ownership — and several millennia of human presence before that.

title: The Jurassic Rocks of Haresfield Hill
summary: Haresfield Beacon is a geological SSSI, exposing Jurassic rock formations considered a classic site of national importance.
body: Haresfield Beacon earned its designation as a geological Site of Special Scientific Interest in 1985, and for good reason. The steep slopes of this promontory expose a sequence of Jurassic rocks that geologists consider a classic reference site of national importance. The Cotswold escarpment is essentially the broken edge of a great slab of limestone, tilted and uplifted over millions of years, and at Haresfield the erosion has cut deep enough to reveal the layered history of a shallow tropical sea that covered this part of England roughly 170 to 200 million years ago. The oolitic limestone that forms the bulk of the Cotswolds — the honey-coloured stone used in every village from Chipping Campden to Bath — was laid down as tiny spherical grains of calcium carbonate rolling on the seabed, accumulating layer upon layer. At Haresfield, different beds of limestone, clay and sandstone are exposed in the cliff faces, each recording changes in sea depth, current and climate over millions of years. The limestone grassland on the summit is itself a product of this geology: the thin, alkaline, free-draining soil supports a flora quite different from the clay vales below. Orchids, rock-rose and wild thyme thrive here precisely because the soil is poor — richer ground would be overwhelmed by coarser grasses. The view from the topograph looks out across the Severn Vale, but the real story lies in the 200-million-year journey from tropical seabed to Cotswold hilltop beneath your feet.

title: A Dead King's Night at Standish
summary: Tradition holds that Edward II's body rested overnight in Standish Church in 1327, on its journey from Berkeley Castle to burial at Gloucester.
body:
On 21 September 1327, the deposed King Edward II was murdered at Berkeley Castle, just twelve miles south of Standish. His body was embalmed and lay in the castle's Chapel of St John for a month before being moved to Gloucester Abbey — now the cathedral — for a lavish royal funeral on 20 December.

Tradition at Standish holds that the funeral cortege stopped overnight at St Nicholas Church, resting the king's body beneath its early fourteenth-century arches. The route makes geographical sense: Standish Lane connects to the old Gloucester road, and the church would have offered a dignified resting place roughly halfway along the journey. Several ancient oaks along Standish Lane are still known as 'The Royal Oaks', said to have been planted to mark the occasion. At least one survivor stands in the field behind Devonia Cottage.

Whether the tradition is literally true remains debated. Some historians note that the body may have travelled by barge along the Severn. But the church itself is beyond dispute: St Nicholas was already old when Edward died, first recorded around 1188, and its pencil-thin spire — visible for miles across the Severn Vale — would have been a landmark for any cortege. The nave and chancel you see today are substantially the same building that stood here in 1327. If the story is true, a murdered king lay beneath these same stone roofs, attended by candlelight, nearly seven hundred years ago.

title: Churchill's Ancestor Lies at Standish
summary: A monument in St Nicholas Church commemorates Sir Henry Winston, whose daughter Sarah married into the Churchill family in 1618, producing a line that led to the wartime Prime Minister.
body:
On the south wall of St Nicholas Church, a painted stone monument commemorates Sir Henry Winston, Lord of the Manor of Standish, who died in February 1609 at the age of forty-seven. His wife Lady Dionise, daughter of Sir George Bond of London, died just weeks later in March, aged forty-four. The couple were buried here, but their story took a peculiar turn.

Sir Henry's daughter Eleanor was refused permission to erect effigies to her parents inside the church. In an act of defiance, she removed not only her parents' carved likenesses but also the effigy of her grandfather Thomas Winston, transporting all three to Long Burton church in Dorset. The architectural frame — pediment, entablature, and columns — was left behind at Standish, empty.

It was another of Sir Henry's daughters who made the family's lasting mark on history. In 1618, Sarah Winston married John Churchill, carrying the Winston name into the Churchill line. Their son, also named Winston Churchill, fathered the first Duke of Marlborough, and from him descended the wartime Prime Minister Sir Winston Spencer-Churchill.

In 1965, the monument was restored to honour this connection, incorporating the Winston and Churchill coats of arms. It was unveiled on 11 January 1966 — almost exactly a year after the great Churchill's death — by his grandson, Winston Churchill MP. The ceremony brought a circle of nearly four centuries to a quiet close, in a fourteenth-century church that the Winstons had once called their own.

title: The Mother Church of a Lost Parish
summary: Standish was one of England's earliest ecclesiastical parishes, formed in 1205, stretching from the Cotswold escarpment to the River Severn.
body:
Stand in the churchyard at St Nicholas and you are at the centre of what was once one of the largest parishes in Gloucestershire. Formed in 1205, Standish was among the earliest ecclesiastical parishes in England, and its boundaries were vast — stretching from the Cotswold escarpment near Whiteshill all the way west to Epney on the River Severn. The parish encompassed Hardwicke, Randwick, Saul, and Colethrop, along with a portion of Pitchcombe village and even a small detached piece of land between Moreton Valence and Whitminster.

As the mother church, St Nicholas answered to Gloucester Abbey, which held the living. The first known reference to the church dates to around 1188, but its role as the hub of such a large territory suggests it existed well before that. By 1229, records name Lawrence as priest of Standish. A succession of graduate vicars followed — Richard of Leigh in 1338, Walter of Evesham in 1345, William Stoke from 1406 to 1427 — suggesting this was considered a prestigious appointment.

Perhaps the most remarkable vicar arrived in 1684: Robert Frampton, the Bishop of Gloucester himself, who took the living at Standish after being deprived of his bishopric as a non-juror — he refused to swear allegiance to William and Mary. The authorities quietly allowed him to keep his Standish vicarage, and he lived out his final years here, dying in 1708. Today the parish is far smaller, its daughter churches long independent, but the building at its heart is largely unchanged since the early fourteenth century.

title: An Island of Stone Left Behind
summary: Cam Long Down is a geological outlier — a fragment of the Cotswold escarpment separated by 1.6 million years of erosion, capped with Jurassic oolitic limestone.
body:
Cam Long Down looks like a ridge that wandered away from the Cotswold escarpment and forgot to go back. In geological terms, that is roughly what happened. The hill is an outlier — a fragment of the escarpment that has been isolated by erosion over the past 1.6 million years during the Quaternary period. It sits on older Lias clays, capped with a layer of Lower Inferior Oolitic limestone that has protected it from wearing away entirely.

The contrast with neighbouring Cam Peak is instructive. Cam Peak has lost its protective limestone cap, exposing the softer Cotteswold Sands beneath, which are eroding into the conical shape you see today. Cam Long Down has kept its cap intact, which is why it remains a long, flat-topped ridge rather than a pointed hill. The two formations together record different stages of the same erosional process.

The Cotswold escarpment probably has one of the highest inland concentrations of landslips in the country, and outliers like Cam Long Down mark where the scarp face once stood. The most westerly former line of the escarpment is traced by Robins Wood Hill and Churchdown Hill, several miles further out into the Severn Vale. Walk the length of Cam Long Down — roughly a mile — and you are walking on a seafloor. The oolitic limestone beneath your boots formed around 170 to 175 million years ago in warm, shallow Jurassic seas. The tiny spherical grains in the rock, called ooliths, are visible to the naked eye. Fossils of sea urchins and ammonites turn up regularly in the exposed stone.

title: The Devil's Wheelbarrow and Cam Peak
summary: Local folklore says the Devil tried to dam the Severn and drown Gloucester, but a quick-thinking cobbler tricked him into dumping his load — creating Cam Peak.
body:
The Devil, so the story goes, was annoyed. There was too much God in Gloucestershire — too many churches, too many bells, and that infuriating cathedral at Gloucester gleaming above the Severn. He decided to fix the problem by damming the river and drowning the entire vale, cathedral and all.

He filled an enormous wheelbarrow with rock and earth from the Cotswold escarpment and set off westward. But somewhere near Cam he met a cobbler walking the other way, carrying a sack of worn-out shoes. The Devil asked how far it was to the Severn. The cobbler — no fool — opened his sack and said he had worn out every pair walking from the river. The distance, he implied, was immense. The Devil lost heart, upended his wheelbarrow, and stomped off. The heap of earth became Cam Peak.

The tale belongs to a family of legends found across southern England. Cley Hill in Wiltshire has an almost identical story, as does Silbury Hill. In each case, the Devil is outwitted by a local tradesman and leaves a conical hill behind. The stories likely arose as folk explanations for hills that looked too regular to be natural — and Cam Peak, with its striking cone shape, certainly fits.
